title: CMV Infection
oneLiner: Opportunistic viral infection causing end-organ disease in immunocompromised (transplant, HIV, cancer); also mononucleosis in immunocompetent. Prompt treatment prevents irreversible damage.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **CMV pneumonitis:** rapidly progressive respiratory failure. *Finding:* bilateral interstitial infiltrates on CXR + hypoxia (SpO₂ <90%) in an immunocompromised patient.  
- **CMV encephalitis:** acute confusion, seizures, brainstem signs. *Finding:* MRI ventriculitis/periventricular enhancement + PCR positive in CSF.  
- **CMV colitis with perforation:** severe abdominal pain, peritonism, haemodynamic instability. *Finding:* free air on erect CXR or CT.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Fever, malaise, myalgia  
  - Organ-specific:  
    - *Pneumonitis:* dry cough, dyspnoea, hypoxia  
    - *Colitis:* watery diarrhoea, haematochezia, abdominal cramps  
    - *Retinitis:* floaters, visual field loss, scotomas  
    - *Oesophagitis:* odynophagia  
    - *Hepatitis:* jaundice, RUQ pain  
    - *Encephalitis:* confusion, lethargy, seizures  
    - *CMV syndrome (transplant):* fever + cytopenias without organ-specific symptoms  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Fundoscopy (retinitis): creamy necrotic lesions with haemorrhages ± retinal detachment  
  - Oral ulcers (less common than HSV)  
  - Abdominal tenderness (colitis)  
  - Crackles (pneumonitis)  
  - Neurological signs (encephalitis)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Immunocompromised (most likely → less likely):**  
  - Other opportunistic infections: PCP (PJP), EBV, HHV-6, Toxoplasma, TB, adenovirus, bacterial pneumonia  
  - Transplant rejection (lung rejection vs. pneumonitis)  
  - Drug fever / drug-induced pneumonitis  
- **Immunocompetent:**  
  - EBV mononucleosis (heterophile +, EBV serology)  
- **Splitter:** *CMV vs. PCP in pneumonitis* – BAL PCR or induced sputum; CMV shows intranuclear inclusions ("owl's eye") on cytology, PCP shows cysts on silver stain.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Fundoscopy (ophthalmology consult if retinitis suspected)  
  - Urgent CXR if pneumonitis  
  - Stool culture + OST if colitis (to exclude bacterial pathogens)  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC (cytopenias – leucopenia, thrombocytopenia)  
  - LFTs (raised transaminases – hepatitis pattern)  
  - Renal function + eGFR (for drug dosing)  
  - CRP (non-specifically elevated)  
  - CMV PCR (quantitative) – key diagnostic  
  - CMV IgM/IgG (primary infection vs. reactivation)  
  - HIV test (in any new CMV end-organ disease)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - *Pneumonitis:* CXR → CT chest (ground-glass opacities, nodules)  
  - *Colitis:* CT abdomen (bowel wall thickening, pneumatosis)  
  - *Encephalitis:* MRI brain (ventriculitis, periventricular enhancement)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - BAL (PCR + cytology + viral culture)  
  - Tissue biopsy (lung, colonic, retinal) – intranuclear inclusions  
  - CSF PCR (if encephalitis)  
- **Diagnostic criteria / thresholds:**  
  - End-organ disease: clinical syndrome + CMV detection in tissue/BAL/CSF by PCR, histology, or culture.  
  - CMV syndrome (transplant): fever + cytopenias + viraemia >1000 copies/mL (check local policy for threshold).  
- **Never-miss test:** CMV PCR in blood for any immunocompromised patient with fever of unknown origin. Also **fundoscopy** in HIV with CD4 <100 cells/µL and visual symptoms.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Ensure airway/breathing/circulation  
  - If pneumonitis: high-flow O₂, consider CPAP/NIV  
  - If encephalitis: treat seizures, CT head to rule out bleed  
  - Start empiric IV ganciclovir after sending relevant tests (PCR, cultures)  
  - Involve microbiology/infectious diseases immediately  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Confirm diagnosis with PCR / histology  
  - Monitor CMV PCR weekly (target undetectable or falling)  
  - Adjust immunosuppression (reduce/stop anti-rejection drugs – consult transplant team)  
  - Supportive care: fluids, O₂, manage diarrhoea  
  - Monitor FBC for ganciclovir-induced neutropenia (consider G-CSF if ANC <500)  
  - If retinitis: involve ophthalmology ± intravitreal ganciclovir  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Secondary prophylaxis: valganciclovir PO (dose per BNF) until CD4 >100 cells/µL for >3 months (HIV) or per transplant protocol (usually 3–6 months)  
  - Primary prophylaxis: indicated in high-risk CMV D+/R– transplants  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - IV fluids (crystalloid, 1–2 L/day if oral intake poor)  
  - VTE prophylaxis – caution if thrombocytopenic (risk of bleeding)  
  - Diet: as tolerated (low residue if colitis)  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly initially
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  - *IV ganciclovir:* 5 mg/kg every 12h (induction) for 14–21 days; then 5 mg/kg daily (maintenance)  
  - *Oral valganciclovir:* 900 mg twice daily (induction); then 900 mg once daily (maintenance)  
  - Both require renal dose adjustment – check BNF per eGFR  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Foscarnet (IV) – nephrotoxic, electrolyte disturbances – reserved for resistant cases  
  - Cidofovir (IV) – nephrotoxic – specialist use only  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Ganciclovir: hypersensitivity, severe neutropenia (ANC <500), thrombocytopenia  
  - Avoid co-administration with other marrow-suppressives (azathioprine, mycophenolate, co-trimoxazole)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Ganciclovir/valganciclovir: dose reduce per eGFR – mandatory  
  - Hepatic: no adjustment needed  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Failing to adjust dose for renal impairment** – leads to toxicity: severe neutropenia, anaemia, seizures. [Check local policy] for exact dosing nomogram.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Bone marrow suppression (neutropenia – risk of sepsis)  
  - Renal impairment (foscarnet/cidofovir)  
  - Seizures (ganciclovir at high doses / renal failure)  
  - Progression of end-organ disease: respiratory failure, bowel perforation, blindness  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Drug resistance (UL97 gene mutation most common)  
  - Allograft dysfunction (rejection, CMV vasculopathy in transplant)  
  - Retinal detachment (retinitis)  
  - Congenital CMV: sensorineural hearing loss, neurodevelopmental delay
sections.Prognosis:
- With appropriate treatment: mortality <10% for most CMV disease (except pneumonitis → 30–50% in severe cases).  
- Relapse common if immunosuppression not reduced.  
- Congenital CMV: long-term deficits in ~10–20% of symptomatic newborns.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check HIV status** in any adult with unexplained CMV end-organ disease.  
- **Monitor CMV PCR weekly** during induction – aim for a falling viral load.  
- **Involve microbiology/infectious diseases early** – also transplant team if relevant.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give ganciclovir** without checking recent FBC and renal function.  
- **Do not use oral valganciclovir** in severe GI disease (e.g., colitis, malabsorption) – use IV.  
- **Do not delay treatment** for retinitis while waiting for ophthalmology – systemic therapy should start immediately.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - New visual floaters, field loss, or eye pain  
  - Worsening dyspnoea or cough  
  - New neurological symptoms (confusion, seizure, focal weakness)  
  - Fever not improving after 72h of treatment  
  - GI bleeding or severe abdominal pain  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Infectious diseases clinic (or transplant team) for PCR monitoring  
  - Ophthalmology (if retinitis) within 1 week  
  - Primary care for general health and drug monitoring
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L  
  - GCS drop (≤13) or seizures  
  - SpO₂ <90% on 15L O₂ or RR >30  
  - Suspected CMV encephalitis with altered consciousness  
  - Haemodynamic instability (hypotension, tachycardia) from GI bleed or sepsis  
- **Specific thresholds:**  
  - Hypoxia (PaO₂ <8 kPa) → need HDU/ITU  
  - Neutrophils <0.5 × 10⁹/L → hold ganciclovir, consider G-CSF, inform senior  
  - CMV DNA >10,000 copies/mL in transplant with clinical deterioration – escalate for consideration of foscarnet/resistance testing

rawMarkdown:
## CMV Infection

**One-liner:** Opportunistic viral infection causing end-organ disease in immunocompromised (transplant, HIV, cancer); also mononucleosis in immunocompetent. Prompt treatment prevents irreversible damage.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **CMV pneumonitis:** rapidly progressive respiratory failure. *Finding:* bilateral interstitial infiltrates on CXR + hypoxia (SpO₂ <90%) in an immunocompromised patient.  
- **CMV encephalitis:** acute confusion, seizures, brainstem signs. *Finding:* MRI ventriculitis/periventricular enhancement + PCR positive in CSF.  
- **CMV colitis with perforation:** severe abdominal pain, peritonism, haemodynamic instability. *Finding:* free air on erect CXR or CT.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Fever, malaise, myalgia  
  - Organ-specific:  
    - *Pneumonitis:* dry cough, dyspnoea, hypoxia  
    - *Colitis:* watery diarrhoea, haematochezia, abdominal cramps  
    - *Retinitis:* floaters, visual field loss, scotomas  
    - *Oesophagitis:* odynophagia  
    - *Hepatitis:* jaundice, RUQ pain  
    - *Encephalitis:* confusion, lethargy, seizures  
    - *CMV syndrome (transplant):* fever + cytopenias without organ-specific symptoms  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Fundoscopy (retinitis): creamy necrotic lesions with haemorrhages ± retinal detachment  
  - Oral ulcers (less common than HSV)  
  - Abdominal tenderness (colitis)  
  - Crackles (pneumonitis)  
  - Neurological signs (encephalitis)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Immunocompromised (most likely → less likely):**  
  - Other opportunistic infections: PCP (PJP), EBV, HHV-6, Toxoplasma, TB, adenovirus, bacterial pneumonia  
  - Transplant rejection (lung rejection vs. pneumonitis)  
  - Drug fever / drug-induced pneumonitis  
- **Immunocompetent:**  
  - EBV mononucleosis (heterophile +, EBV serology)  
- **Splitter:** *CMV vs. PCP in pneumonitis* – BAL PCR or induced sputum; CMV shows intranuclear inclusions ("owl's eye") on cytology, PCP shows cysts on silver stain.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - Fundoscopy (ophthalmology consult if retinitis suspected)  
  - Urgent CXR if pneumonitis  
  - Stool culture + OST if colitis (to exclude bacterial pathogens)  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC (cytopenias – leucopenia, thrombocytopenia)  
  - LFTs (raised transaminases – hepatitis pattern)  
  - Renal function + eGFR (for drug dosing)  
  - CRP (non-specifically elevated)  
  - CMV PCR (quantitative) – key diagnostic  
  - CMV IgM/IgG (primary infection vs. reactivation)  
  - HIV test (in any new CMV end-organ disease)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - *Pneumonitis:* CXR → CT chest (ground-glass opacities, nodules)  
  - *Colitis:* CT abdomen (bowel wall thickening, pneumatosis)  
  - *Encephalitis:* MRI brain (ventriculitis, periventricular enhancement)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - BAL (PCR + cytology + viral culture)  
  - Tissue biopsy (lung, colonic, retinal) – intranuclear inclusions  
  - CSF PCR (if encephalitis)  
- **Diagnostic criteria / thresholds:**  
  - End-organ disease: clinical syndrome + CMV detection in tissue/BAL/CSF by PCR, histology, or culture.  
  - CMV syndrome (transplant): fever + cytopenias + viraemia >1000 copies/mL (check local policy for threshold).  
- **Never-miss test:** CMV PCR in blood for any immunocompromised patient with fever of unknown origin. Also **fundoscopy** in HIV with CD4 <100 cells/µL and visual symptoms.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Ensure airway/breathing/circulation  
  - If pneumonitis: high-flow O₂, consider CPAP/NIV  
  - If encephalitis: treat seizures, CT head to rule out bleed  
  - Start empiric IV ganciclovir after sending relevant tests (PCR, cultures)  
  - Involve microbiology/infectious diseases immediately  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Confirm diagnosis with PCR / histology  
  - Monitor CMV PCR weekly (target undetectable or falling)  
  - Adjust immunosuppression (reduce/stop anti-rejection drugs – consult transplant team)  
  - Supportive care: fluids, O₂, manage diarrhoea  
  - Monitor FBC for ganciclovir-induced neutropenia (consider G-CSF if ANC <500)  
  - If retinitis: involve ophthalmology ± intravitreal ganciclovir  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Secondary prophylaxis: valganciclovir PO (dose per BNF) until CD4 >100 cells/µL for >3 months (HIV) or per transplant protocol (usually 3–6 months)  
  - Primary prophylaxis: indicated in high-risk CMV D+/R– transplants  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - IV fluids (crystalloid, 1–2 L/day if oral intake poor)  
  - VTE prophylaxis – caution if thrombocytopenic (risk of bleeding)  
  - Diet: as tolerated (low residue if colitis)  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly initially

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**  
  - *IV ganciclovir:* 5 mg/kg every 12h (induction) for 14–21 days; then 5 mg/kg daily (maintenance)  
  - *Oral valganciclovir:* 900 mg twice daily (induction); then 900 mg once daily (maintenance)  
  - Both require renal dose adjustment – check BNF per eGFR  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Foscarnet (IV) – nephrotoxic, electrolyte disturbances – reserved for resistant cases  
  - Cidofovir (IV) – nephrotoxic – specialist use only  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Ganciclovir: hypersensitivity, severe neutropenia (ANC <500), thrombocytopenia  
  - Avoid co-administration with other marrow-suppressives (azathioprine, mycophenolate, co-trimoxazole)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Ganciclovir/valganciclovir: dose reduce per eGFR – mandatory  
  - Hepatic: no adjustment needed  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Failing to adjust dose for renal impairment** – leads to toxicity: severe neutropenia, anaemia, seizures. [Check local policy] for exact dosing nomogram.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Bone marrow suppression (neutropenia – risk of sepsis)  
  - Renal impairment (foscarnet/cidofovir)  
  - Seizures (ganciclovir at high doses / renal failure)  
  - Progression of end-organ disease: respiratory failure, bowel perforation, blindness  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Drug resistance (UL97 gene mutation most common)  
  - Allograft dysfunction (rejection, CMV vasculopathy in transplant)  
  - Retinal detachment (retinitis)  
  - Congenital CMV: sensorineural hearing loss, neurodevelopmental delay

### Prognosis
- With appropriate treatment: mortality <10% for most CMV disease (except pneumonitis → 30–50% in severe cases).  
- Relapse common if immunosuppression not reduced.  
- Congenital CMV: long-term deficits in ~10–20% of symptomatic newborns.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check HIV status** in any adult with unexplained CMV end-organ disease.  
- **Monitor CMV PCR weekly** during induction – aim for a falling viral load.  
- **Involve microbiology/infectious diseases early** – also transplant team if relevant.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give ganciclovir** without checking recent FBC and renal function.  
- **Do not use oral valganciclovir** in severe GI disease (e.g., colitis, malabsorption) – use IV.  
- **Do not delay treatment** for retinitis while waiting for ophthalmology – systemic therapy should start immediately.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - New visual floaters, field loss, or eye pain  
  - Worsening dyspnoea or cough  
  - New neurological symptoms (confusion, seizure, focal weakness)  
  - Fever not improving after 72h of treatment  
  - GI bleeding or severe abdominal pain  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Infectious diseases clinic (or transplant team) for PCR monitoring  
  - Ophthalmology (if retinitis) within 1 week  
  - Primary care for general health and drug monitoring

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L  
  - GCS drop (≤13) or seizures  
  - SpO₂ <90% on 15L O₂ or RR >30  
  - Suspected CMV encephalitis with altered consciousness  
  - Haemodynamic instability (hypotension, tachycardia) from GI bleed or sepsis  
- **Specific thresholds:**  
  - Hypoxia (PaO₂ <8 kPa) → need HDU/ITU  
  - Neutrophils <0.5 × 10⁹/L → hold ganciclovir, consider G-CSF, inform senior  
  - CMV DNA >10,000 copies/mL in transplant with clinical deterioration – escalate for consideration of foscarnet/resistance testing
